title_en: Legacy and Position in Muslim Commentarial Tradition
title_ar: الإرث والمكانة في التقليد الشرحي الإسلامي
content_en:
Ikmal al-Mu'lim occupies an important position in the history of commentary on Sahih Muslim as the most significant pre-Nawawi commentary on the collection and the primary source through which the western Maliki reading of the collection was transmitted to subsequent generations. Qadi Iyad's commentary was the standard scholarly reference on Sahih Muslim in Maliki circles from the time of its composition until it was complemented — though not displaced for Maliki purposes — by an-Nawawi's Al-Minhaj in the following century.

An-Nawawi's use of Ikmal al-Mu'lim as a primary source for Al-Minhaj has ensured that Qadi Iyad's scholarship has continued to influence Sahih Muslim studies even for scholars who primarily use the later commentary. An-Nawawi cites Qadi Iyad extensively throughout Al-Minhaj, sometimes agreeing and sometimes disagreeing with his positions, but always treating him as a major authority whose views must be addressed. This integration of Qadi Iyad's scholarship into Al-Minhaj is one of the mechanisms through which the western Maliki tradition has contributed to the broader Islamic scholarly heritage.

The influence of Qadi Iyad on subsequent Maliki scholarship in North Africa and Andalusia has been immense. His works — Ikmal al-Mu'lim alongside Ash-Shifa, the Tartib al-Madarik, and his other writings — were central texts in the Maliki educational tradition of the Maghreb and remained so through the Ottoman period and into the modern era. The veneration in which Qadi Iyad is held in Morocco, Tunisia, and other Maliki-majority regions reflects both the quality of his scholarship and the enduring influence of the tradition he represents.

For contemporary scholars of the Maliki tradition and of hadith commentary in general, Ikmal al-Mu'lim is an essential resource that rewards careful study. Its preservation of the western Maliki reading of Sahih Muslim, its linguistic scholarship, and its engagement with the Ash'ari theological tradition all make it a work of lasting importance that stands alongside Al-Minhaj as one of the two great commentaries on the collection of Imam Muslim.
